126	type watchersMap map[int]*cacheWatcher
127	
128	func (wm watchersMap) addWatcher(w *cacheWatcher, number int) {
129		wm[number] = w
130	}
131	
132	func (wm watchersMap) deleteWatcher(number int) {
133		delete(wm, number)
134	}
135	
136	func (wm watchersMap) terminateAll(done func(*cacheWatcher)) {
137		for key, watcher := range wm {
138			delete(wm, key)
139			done(watcher)
140		}
141	}

143	type indexedWatchers struct {
144		allWatchers   map[namespacedName]watchersMap
145		valueWatchers map[string]watchersMap
146	}
147	
148	func (i *indexedWatchers) addWatcher(w *cacheWatcher, number int, scope namespacedName, value string, supported bool) {
149		if supported {
150			if _, ok := i.valueWatchers[value]; !ok {
151				i.valueWatchers[value] = watchersMap{}
152			}
153			i.valueWatchers[value].addWatcher(w, number)
154		} else {
155			scopedWatchers, ok := i.allWatchers[scope]
156			if !ok {
157				scopedWatchers = watchersMap{}
158				i.allWatchers[scope] = scopedWatchers
159			}
160			scopedWatchers.addWatcher(w, number)
161		}
162	}
163	
164	func (i *indexedWatchers) deleteWatcher(number int, scope namespacedName, value string, supported bool) {
165		if supported {
166			i.valueWatchers[value].deleteWatcher(number)
167			if len(i.valueWatchers[value]) == 0 {
168				delete(i.valueWatchers, value)
169			}
170		} else {
171			i.allWatchers[scope].deleteWatcher(number)
172			if len(i.allWatchers[scope]) == 0 {
173				delete(i.allWatchers, scope)
174			}
175		}
176	}
177	
178	func (i *indexedWatchers) terminateAll(groupResource schema.GroupResource, done func(*cacheWatcher)) {
179		// note that we don't have to call setDrainInputBufferLocked method on the watchers
180		// because we take advantage of the default value - stop immediately
181		// also watchers that have had already its draining strategy set
182		// are no longer available (they were removed from the allWatchers and the valueWatchers maps)
183		if len(i.allWatchers) > 0 || len(i.valueWatchers) > 0 {
184			klog.Warningf("Terminating all watchers from cacher %v", groupResource)
185		}
186		for _, watchers := range i.allWatchers {
187			watchers.terminateAll(done)
188		}
189		for _, watchers := range i.valueWatchers {
190			watchers.terminateAll(done)
191		}
192		i.allWatchers = map[namespacedName]watchersMap{}
193		i.valueWatchers = map[string]watchersMap{}
194	}

196	// As we don't need a high precision here, we keep all watchers timeout within a
197	// second in a bucket, and pop up them once at the timeout. To be more specific,
198	// if you set fire time at X, you can get the bookmark within (X-1,X+1) period.
199	type watcherBookmarkTimeBuckets struct {
200		// the key of watcherBuckets is the number of seconds since createTime
201		watchersBuckets   map[int64][]*cacheWatcher
202		createTime        time.Time
203		startBucketID     int64
204		clock             clock.Clock
205		bookmarkFrequency time.Duration
206	}
207	
208	func newTimeBucketWatchers(clock clock.Clock, bookmarkFrequency time.Duration) *watcherBookmarkTimeBuckets {
209		return &watcherBookmarkTimeBuckets{
210			watchersBuckets:   make(map[int64][]*cacheWatcher),
211			createTime:        clock.Now(),

## Report: Kubernetes APIServer Watch Cache Implementation

**Central Type:** `Cacher`

**File Path:** `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/cacher.go`

**Package Directory:** `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/`

---

**How the Watch Cache Reduces etcd Load:**

The `Cacher` implements a shared single-watch pattern to minimize etcd load. A single `Reflector` maintains one ListAndWatch connection to etcd, pulling all events into the `watchCache` (a sliding-window event history with configurable capacity up to 100k events, retained for a minimum ~75-second duration). Client WATCH/LIST/GET requests are served directly from this in-memory cache rather than hitting etcd. When an event arrives from etcd, it's broadcast to all connected client watchers via the `dispatchEvent()` method, which routes the single stream from etcd to multiple watchers without creating new etcd connections. ResourceVersion semantics enforce consistency: clients specify a starting ResourceVersion, and the cache either serves cached events back-to-buffered from the history, or waits for the cache to catch up to the requested version.

---

**Related Supporting Types:**

1. **`watchCache`** (`watch_cache.go`): The in-memory sliding-window ring buffer that stores `watchCacheEvent` objects. Maintains current state via a `store.Indexer` and event history with dynamic capacity bounds. Implements indexing to accelerate LIST filtering.

2. **`cacheWatcher`** (`cache_watcher.go`): Represents a single client watch connection. Receives events from the shared `watchCache` via an input channel and filters/forwards them based on predicates (labels/fields). Manages ResourceVersion synchronization and bookmark events.

3. **`Reflector`** (from `client-go/tools/cache`): Runs the single ListAndWatch against etcd, populating the `watchCache` with all state changes. The Cacher wraps this to provide the facade that transparently serves many clients from one etcd subscription.

# Apiserver Watch Cache — How it Reduces etcd Load

## Context
The user asked a knowledge question about the Kubernetes apiserver: how the
watch cache reduces etcd load, plus the central type that implements it and
the file it lives in. This plan answers the question directly; no code changes
are required.

## Answer

### Central type and file
- **Type:** `Cacher`
- **File:** `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/cacher.go`
  (declared at line 263, doc comment at lines 258–262).
- **Package:** `k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher`

The in-tree doc comment summarizes its job:

> "Cacher is responsible for serving WATCH and LIST requests for a given
> resource from its internal cache and updating its cache in the background
> based on the underlying storage contents. Cacher implements storage.Interface
> (although most of the calls are just delegated to the underlying storage)."

### How it reduces etcd load

1. **Single shared watch to etcd, fan-out to many clients.** The `Cacher`
   runs one `Reflector` (from `client-go/tools/cache`) that performs a single
   `ListAndWatch` against etcd for the resource. All API clients that issue
   WATCH requests on that resource are multiplexed off this one etcd
   subscription via `dispatchEvent`, instead of each opening its own etcd
   watch. N client watches collapse to 1 etcd watch.

2. **In-memory event history (sliding ring buffer).** A companion type
   `watchCache` (in
   `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/watch_cache.go`) keeps a
   bounded ring buffer of recent `watchCacheEvent`s plus the current object
   set in a `store.Indexer`. When a client reconnects with a
   `resourceVersion`, the cacher replays missed events from this buffer
   instead of asking etcd to re-stream them. `DefaultEventFreshDuration`
   (`defaultBookmarkFrequency + 15s`, ~75s) controls how long history is
   retained; capacity scales dynamically up to ~100k events.

3. **LIST/GET served from cache.** When `ResourceVersionMatch` semantics
   allow it, LIST and GET go straight to the in-memory indexer rather than
   etcd, which is the dominant traffic class for controllers doing periodic
   resyncs.

4. **Bookmarks keep clients within the history window.** Periodic bookmark
   events (every ~1 min) advance each watcher's resourceVersion, so a brief
   client disconnect almost always reconnects within the cached history and
   never falls back to a fresh etcd LIST.

### Closely related types
- `watchCache` — `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/watch_cache.go`
  (the ring buffer + current-state indexer).
- `cacheWatcher` — `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/cache_watcher.go`
  (one per client WATCH; receives dispatched events and applies
  label/field predicates).
- `Reflector` — `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/reflector.go`
  (drives the single ListAndWatch against etcd that feeds `watchCache`).

## Verification
Open `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/cacher.go` and read the
doc comment at lines 258–262 plus the `Cacher` struct at line 263. Cross-check
the dispatch path (`dispatchEvent`) and the `watchCache` ring buffer in
`watch_cache.go` to see the single-etcd-watch / many-client-watch fan-out.

the file it lives in. This plan answers the question directly; no code changes
are required.

## Answer

### Central type and file
- **Type:** `Cacher`
- **File:** `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/cacher.go`
  (declared at line 263, doc comment at lines 258–262).
- **Package:** `k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher`

The in-tree doc comment summarizes its job:

> "Cacher is responsible for serving WATCH and LIST requests for a given
> resource from its internal cache and updating its cache in the background
> based on the underlying storage contents. Cacher implements storage.Interface
> (although most of the calls are just delegated to the underlying storage)."

### How it reduces etcd load

1. **Single shared watch to etcd, fan-out to many clients.** The `Cacher`
   runs one `Reflector` (from `client-go/tools/cache`) that performs a single
   `ListAndWatch` against etcd for the resource. All API clients that issue
   WATCH requests on that resource are multiplexed off this one etcd
   subscription via `dispatchEvent`, instead of each opening its own etcd
   watch. N client watches collapse to 1 etcd watch.

2. **In-memory event history (sliding ring buffer).** A companion type
   `watchCache` (in
   `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/watch_cache.go`) keeps a
   bounded ring buffer of recent `watchCacheEvent`s plus the current object
   set in a `store.Indexer`. When a client reconnects with a
   `resourceVersion`, the cacher replays missed events from this buffer
   instead of asking etcd to re-stream them. `DefaultEventFreshDuration`
   (`defaultBookmarkFrequency + 15s`, ~75s) controls how long history is
   retained; capacity scales dynamically up to ~100k events.

3. **LIST/GET served from cache.** When `ResourceVersionMatch` semantics
   allow it, LIST and GET go straight to the in-memory indexer rather than
   etcd, which is the dominant traffic class for controllers doing periodic
   resyncs.

4. **Bookmarks keep clients within the history window.** Periodic bookmark
   events (every ~1 min) advance each watcher's resourceVersion, so a brief
   client disconnect almost always reconnects within the cached history and
   never falls back to a fresh etcd LIST.

### Closely related types
- `watchCache` — `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/watch_cache.go`
  (the ring buffer + current-state indexer).
- `cacheWatcher` — `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/cache_watcher.go`
  (one per client WATCH; receives dispatched events and applies
  label/field predicates).
- `Reflector` — `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/reflector.go`
  (drives the single ListAndWatch against etcd that feeds `watchCache`).
